description: IoT and robotics — ROS2 nodes, MQTT sensor networks, robot controllers, network device interfaces, and edge deployment. Code runs in Docker during development; ultimately executes on physical hardware.
roadmap:
0. Foundation skills: event-storming → define-bounded-contexts → ubiquitous-language
1. Choose framework: ros2-python (ROS2 Jazzy) or mqtt-only (pure MQTT/IoT)
2. Scaffold workspace and verify build (scaffold-ros2-workspace)
3. Create first node or device interface (create-ros2-node)
4. Add simulation layer for hardware-free testing (ros2-simulation)
5. Connect MQTT broker if IoT integration needed (connect-mqtt-broker)
6. Add sensor interfaces with mock/real abstraction (add-sensor-interface)
7. Implement controller with mandatory safety checks (write-robot-controller)
8. Add network device monitoring if applicable (add-network-device-interface)
9. Deploy to edge hardware (deploy-to-edge)

notes.hardware_gap: The workbench scaffolds and simulates, but cannot actuate a physical robot or read a real sensor. Every skill separates what runs in Docker (simulation, mocks) from what requires physical hardware.
notes.real_time: Sub-10ms control loops require PREEMPT_RT kernel and dedicated hardware. Docker on a dev laptop cannot guarantee this. Flag RT requirements early; design for simulation first.
notes.safety: Robot controllers must implement velocity clamping, e-stop, and watchdog timers as mandatory — not optional. The write-robot-controller skill treats these as non-negotiable.
notes.ros2_version: ROS2 Jazzy Jalisco (LTS, supported until May 2029)
notes.simulation: Mock-only — abstract interface pattern instead of full Gazebo. Keeps the workbench dependency-free; no extra containers needed.
notes.language: Python primary. C++ scaffold available but skills are Python-first.
